Basics — five minutes to a clade run

Shinichi Nakagawa

Source: vignettes/basics.Rmd
basics.Rmd

What this vignette is for

The shortest path from a fresh install to a clade run you can read. Five short sections, each anchored on one of the four core functions (default_specs(), run_alife(), get_run_data(), plot_run()) and a closing pointer to the deeper material. If you want narrative motivation, the architectural picture, or the lab vision behind clade, read vignette("getting-started") first; this vignette assumes you just want a working run.

1. Start from default_specs()

default_specs() is the single source of truth for every parameter clade understands. It takes no arguments and returns a named list of ~300 fields with sensible defaults — sparse 30 × 30 grid, 50 starting agents, 500-tick run, BNN brain, diploid sex, no optional modules (no disease, no kin selection, no predators, etc.). The discipline across the codebase is always copy from default_specs() and modify the copy; never hand-build a specs list from scratch, because new modules add fields over time and you would miss them.

library(clade)

specs <- default_specs()       # the canonical starting list
specs$n_agents_init <- 100L    # one common change
specs$max_ticks     <- 200L    # another
specs$random_seed   <- 42L     # reproducibility

To see what changed from defaults, use print_specs(specs, diff_only = TRUE). To see every parameter grouped by biological theme, use print_specs(specs) (no arguments needed). To see what biological modules are off-by-default (the opt-in design), look at vignette("parameter-reference") or read the field-by-field roxygen in ?default_specs.

2. Run the simulation with run_alife()

run_alife(specs) is the one call you need. Internally it starts a Julia session on the first invocation (one-time ~60-90 s JIT cost), sends specs across the R-Julia boundary once, runs every tick in Julia, and returns an env list containing the surviving agents, per-tick statistics, per-death records, and (if you set specs$log_genomes = TRUE) per-tick genome snapshots. After the run, env$viability carries the verdict from viability_report() and the function emits a warning() if the population crashed — so silent collapses can’t slip past you. Pass verbose = FALSE for batch / test usage; the viability warning still fires.

env <- run_alife(specs)        # one boundary crossing, one return
env$t                          # the final tick (== specs$max_ticks)
env$viability$verdict          # "viable", "weak", or "crashed"

run_clade() is an alias for run_alife() if you prefer the package name in your scripts. For multi-seed replicates use batch_seeds(specs, seeds = 1:5); for a list of different specs use batch_alife(list_of_specs, n_cores = 4L) (each worker pays the Julia startup cost separately, so parallelism is worth it from roughly 20 scenarios upward).

3. Extract the run with get_run_data()

get_run_data(env) reshapes the run into two tidy data frames ($ticks and $deaths) plus a third ($genomes) that is NULL unless you set specs$log_genomes = TRUE. $ticks has one row per logged tick and ~60 population columns — the always-present core (t, n_agents, n_births, n_deaths, mean_energy, mean_age, mean_body_size, genetic_diversity, grass_coverage, …) plus module-specific columns that are zero when the corresponding module is off, so the shape is stable across specs. $deaths has one row per agent death with seven columns (id, t, age, energy, cause, body_size, num_offspring); inspecting $deaths$cause is the fastest way to see whether a run was starvation-dominated, age-dominated, or (when predators are on) predation-dominated.

rd <- get_run_data(env)
head(rd$ticks)
tail(rd$ticks[, c("t", "n_agents", "mean_energy", "genetic_diversity")])
table(rd$deaths$cause)

For the full column list of a specific run, call colnames(rd$ticks) — the canonical schema lives in inst/julia/src/logging.jl (_init_progress). For genome snapshots, use get_genome_data(env) (same $genomes long-format data frame, plus reserved placeholders for heterozygosity and FST).

4. Plot the run with plot_run()

plot_run(rd) returns a 2 × 3 dashboard (a patchwork object) of six time-series diagnostics: population size, mean energy ± 1 SD, genetic diversity, births vs deaths per tick, grass coverage, and a sixth panel that switches between BNN prior sigma (when the BNN brain’s plasticity is varying — the Baldwin Effect panel after Baldwin 1896 / Hinton & Nowlan 1987) and mean body size (when sigma is flat or absent). It’s the fastest way to read whether a run is biologically sensible: a healthy run shows population holding above a viability floor, energy stable or oscillating, diversity not collapsing to zero, births roughly tracking deaths after burn-in, and grass not stripped to bare ground. If any of those panels look pathological (population to zero, energy collapsing, diversity to zero in <50 ticks), the run probably won’t support trait-mean interpretations — and env$viability$verdict will already say so.

plot_run(rd)

plot_run() is intentionally robust to the unhappy paths: it errors with a clear message if rd$ticks is missing, returns a ggplot placeholder (rather than crashing) if $ticks has zero rows, and quietly truncates the timeline when run_alife() crashed mid-run (the post-crash zero-padded rows are filtered by t > 0). So you can pipe a viability-flagged crashed run straight into plot_run() and read the partial story.

5. What next?

Once you can read plot_run() you have most of clade’s surface. Where to go from here, in order of investment:

  • Multi-seed replicates. Every conclusion in clade should be defended at ≥ 5 seeds; one run is a story, five is data. batch_seeds(specs, seeds = 1:5) runs the same specs across seeds; pair with viability_report() per replicate and keep only the viable ones.
  • Scenario vignettes. vignette(package = "clade") lists ~40 worked scenarios under s-* (scenarios) and paper-* (paper reproductions). Start with vignette("s-baseline") for the shortest fully-narrated run, then vignette("scenarios") for the full catalogue.
  • Paper reproductions. If you have a behavioural-ecology paper with a quantitative prediction, the paper-template.Rmd vignette shows how to turn it into a clade experiment. The published reproductions (Wolf 2007, Trivers 1971, Réale 2010, Griesser 2023, Ryan 1990, …) each follow the same template and report what reproduces vs what doesn’t.
  • Parameter-space search. When you want the best (or most diverse) configurations rather than a single run, search_map_elites() / search_cmaes() / search_viability() run optimisation over the spec list. vignette("ps-introduction") is the starting point.
  • Narrative depth. For the architectural picture, the four brain types, the R-Julia split, the lab values, and the vision: vignette("getting-started") and dev/design/00-vision.md.

On this page
